g.data-narration: OOP organizes code around objects that bundle data and behavior, revolutionizing software development.
g.text: Object-Oriented Programming is a paradigm that organizes
code around "objects" rather than functions. Objects bundle

g.data-narration: The four pillars of OOP are Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction.
Encapsulation bundles data and methods into a single unit, restricting direct access to prevent misuse.
g.text: Definition: The mechanism of bundling data (variables) and methods
that operate on the data into a single unit called a class.
Also restricts direct access to object's components - prevents
accidental interference and misuse of data.

g.data-narration: Private variables use public getters and setters for controlled access, ensuring data integrity.
Inheritance allows a derived class to acquire properties and behaviors from a base class, promoting reuse.
g.text: Definition: Mechanism by which a class (derived/child) inherits properties
and behaviors from another class (base/parent). Promotes code reusability
and establishes a natural hierarchy between classes.

g.data-narration: There are five types of inheritance: Single, Multiple, Multilevel, Hierarchical, and Hybrid.
The extends keyword creates inheritance - the Dog class gains eat() from Animal while adding bark().
Polymorphism means 'many forms' - objects can be treated as instances of their parent class.
g.text: Definition: Ability of objects to take multiple forms. Enables one interface
to be used for a general class of actions, with specific action determined
by the exact nature of the situation at runtime.

g.data-narration: Compile-time polymorphism uses method overloading - same name, different parameters within the same class.
Runtime polymorphism uses method overriding - the actual object type determines which method executes.
Abstraction hides complex implementation details, showing only essential features to reduce complexity.
g.text: Definition: Process of hiding complex implementation details and showing
only essential features of an object. Helps reduce programming complexity
and effort by focusing on what an object does instead of how it does it.

g.data-narration: Abstract classes can have concrete methods; interfaces define contracts. Use abstract for inheritance, interfaces for polymorphism.
Encapsulation hides data, abstraction hides complexity. Use private for encapsulation, abstract classes for partial implementation.
Remember: Encapsulation bundles, Inheritance acquires, Polymorphism has many forms, Abstraction hides complexity.
